title: Chapter 36: Penthouse Negotiations
content: The wine was real. Marcus hadn't expected that.

He swirled it in the glass — actual crystal, because of course — and watched Vivienne Duchamps rebuild herself across the coffee table, piece by piece, with the methodical precision of someone who'd been deconstructed and reassembled many times before. The color was returning to her cheeks. Her hands had stopped trembling. The mask was back, but it was the evening mask now, not the battle mask — the one worn for dinner parties and negotiations, not sieges.

The servants had eaten too. Marcus had produced a second System meal — simpler, heartier, a stew with root vegetables and thick bread — and watched the seven gaunt figures eat with the desperate restraint of people who knew that eating too fast would make them sick. Elena had supervised, doling out portions, checking vitals, performing the quiet triage she was born for. Two of them — an older woman and a young man who couldn't have been older than nineteen — were in bad shape. Elena had started IV fluids on both within ten minutes.
That left Marcus alone with Vivienne, the crystal, and a silence that demanded to be filled with something other than the wind against the penthouse windows.
"You're not what I expected," Vivienne said, setting down her glass. The wine had loosened her — not much, but enough. The vowel sounds were slightly rounder, the consonants slightly less sharp.
"What did you expect?"

"Another raider. Another brute with a gun and a hard-on for power." She examined him with those dark, assessing eyes. "You're something else. Your food appears from nowhere. Your zombie woman killed three of Crane's scouts with her bare hands. Your Japanese martial artist moves through the streets like a ghost. You're not from around here, Marcus Chen, and I don't mean New York."
The name landed like a small revelation. She'd been paying attention. She'd known who he was before he walked through her door.

"Word travels," she confirmed, reading his expression. "Crane's been asking about you. His scouts have been reporting to him — a man who conjures food, travels with a zombie queen, and walks through hostile territory like he owns it. He's *fascinated*." The word dripped with contempt. "Victor Crane is fascinated by very few things. It makes him dangerous."
Marcus leaned forward. "Tell me about Crane."
"What's it worth to you?"
"Same terms as before. Information for supplies. Ongoing partnership."
She almost smiled. Almost. "You're learning."

Then she stood, crossed to the far wall, and pressed a hidden panel. A cabinet slid open, revealing something Marcus hadn't expected: a radio. Not a toy, not a walkie-talkie — a shortwave radio with a padded headset, a hand-crank generator, and a stack of coded frequency charts beside it. Military-grade, pre-outbreak, the kind of equipment that cost six figures and required licenses that money alone couldn't buy.

"Where did you get that?" Elena asked from across the room, where she'd been adjusting the young man's IV. Her voice carried the particular interest of someone who recognized strategic value when she saw it.

"Philippe — my late husband — was a communications contractor. Government contracts. He installed this before the outbreak as part of a 'preparedness initiative' that I thought was paranoid nonsense." Vivienne's jaw tightened briefly at the memory. "He died on Day Three, trying to reach the hospital. The radio has been sitting here, mostly useless, because there was no one to talk to."
"Mostly useless?"

"Mostly." She ran her fingers along the frequency charts. "But not entirely. Two weeks ago, I started picking up fragments. Broken transmissions. Half-words in the static. I thought it was interference — Crane's radios, military remnants, random noise. But it wasn't random."

She picked up a leather-bound notebook from the cabinet and opened it to a page dense with handwritten notes. Times. Frequencies. Fragments of text transcribed with the painstaking care of someone who'd been solving puzzles her whole life.

*"—CUPID PROTOCOL—active—failure rate—"*
*"—evolution exceeds—parameters—"*
*"—correction deployed—awaiting confirmation—"*
*"—the System is not the error. The System is the—"*
The last fragment cut off mid-sentence. Marcus felt his pulse spike.

Cupid Protocol. The same term that had appeared in junk data from the System, the same phrase that Sera's fragmented memories sometimes brushed against like a tongue probing a sore tooth. Project Cupid. It was real. It wasn't a System artifact or a fever dream — it was a *signal*, broadcast from somewhere, received by this woman in her penthouse while she starved and watched the world burn.
"Who's broadcasting this?" His voice came out harder than he intended.

"I don't know. The fragments are too broken to triangulate a source, and I'm not an engineer." Vivienne's eyes met his, and for the first time he saw something beneath the aristocratic armor that wasn't pride. It was *fear*. "But I know what else I've heard. Other survivors. There's a network — small, scattered, hidden in the mountains north of the city. They call themselves the Relay. They've been broadcasting on encrypted channels, trying to coordinate, trying to figure out what happened. They've picked up Crane's communications too, and they've been tracking his movements."

Marcus's mind was racing. A survivor network in the mountains. Encrypted communications. And Cupid Protocol fragments — the first breadcrumb leading back to whoever had created the virus, the System, the whole catastrophic architecture of the apocalypse.
"What have they learned about Crane?"
Vivienne's expression sharpened. She flipped to another page in the notebook — maps, hand-drawn, annotated with dates and troop movements.

"Crane doesn't just raid. He *conquers*. Every building, every block, every survivor group — he absorbs them systematically. First the scouts, then the siege, then the ultimatum: join or die. He's taken fourteen buildings in the last three weeks, added a hundred and twenty fighters to his army, and he's not slowing down." She tapped the map. "Here. This is his next target."
Marcus looked at the annotation. His stomach dropped.
New Eden. She'd marked the titanium fortress with a red circle and a date.

"One week," Vivienne said quietly. "His scouts have been probing your perimeter for three days. He knows where you are. He knows about the generator, the food production, the zombie queen. And he's coming."

The room went silent except for the hiss of the IV and the wind against the glass. One week. Seven days until Victor Crane's two-hundred-strong raider army descended on New Eden and everything Marcus had built — the fortress, the safe zone, the community of eighty-seven people who'd trusted him with their lives — became another conquest in a warlord's expanding kingdom.
And Lily was still in that panic room. Four days of air. Maybe less.

Dual urgency. The word crystallized in his mind with the cold clarity of危机. He had to rescue Lily *and* prepare New Eden for an attack, and the timelines overlapped in the worst possible way. If they spent four days extracting Lily, they'd have three days to fortify against an army. If they stayed to fortify, Lily would suffocate.
"Elena," he said. "We need to get this intelligence back to Sera. Now."
Elena was already reaching for her radio, then stopped. The range. The hand unit couldn't reach forty floors through raider-jammed frequencies. They'd need to go back down.

"Wait," Vivienne said. She was looking at the radio, then at the notebook, then at the door of the penthouse where her seven servants — her *people*, Marcus corrected himself, because whatever she called them, they were her people — sat or stood or leaned against walls, watching with eyes that had been fed and were now hungry for something else entirely.

She looked at the old woman, Margaux, who'd been with the household for thirty years. At the young man, Henri, who'd been a busboy at one of Philippe's restaurants before the world ended. At the others — the cook, the driver, the gardener, the maid, the security man — all of them thin and broken and loyal beyond reason.
"Margaux," Vivienne said. "How long have you worked for this family?"
"Twenty-nine years, Madame," the old woman said without hesitation.
"And if I told you we were leaving — leaving this tower, leaving the city — would you come?"
The question hung in the air. Margaux's eyes went wide, then filled with something that made Marcus look away because it was too raw to witness.
"Where you go, Madame, I go."
"Henri?"
The young man straightened. "Oui, Madame. Always."

One by one, they answered. The cook, the driver, the gardener, the maid, the security man. *Oui, Madame. Always. Where you go.* Seven people who'd stayed through starvation and horror because Vivienne Duchamps had given them a structure to inhabit, and they would rather inhabit it in hell than wander free in the apocalypse.

Vivienne turned back to Marcus, and for the first time since he'd walked into the penthouse, there was no mask. No armor. Just a woman with dark circles under her eyes and tear tracks still faintly visible on her cheeks and seven people behind her who would follow her into the grave and needed her to lead them somewhere other than death.

"I would like to come to New Eden," she said. Not demanded. Not negotiated. *Asked*. The word was quiet, controlled, dignified — and underneath it was the same raw fracture he'd heard when she'd said *please*, the sound of pride bending without breaking, reshaping itself into something that could survive the request.
"Please," she added, and the second *please* cost her more than the first, because the first had escaped by accident and this one was *chosen*, deliberate, a door she was opening with her own hand.

**[QUEST COMPLETE: The Proud Heart]**
**[Vivienne Duchamps has asked for help. Not demanded. Asked.]**
**[Affection: 10 → 35]**
**[Relationship Status: Trusted Partner]**
**[REWARDS DELIVERED:]**
**[- Item Acquired: Shortwave Radio Array — Military-grade communications equipment. Range: 200+ miles. Encrypted frequency capability. Can contact the Relay network in the mountains.]**
**[- New Party Member: Vivienne Duchamps — Communications Officer]**
**[- Party Role: Intelligence Specialist — Vivienne's surveillance data provides +30% threat awareness in urban environments]**
**[- Personnel: 7 survivors (Duchamps household) — Available for community integration]**
Marcus stood up and extended his hand. "Welcome to the partnership, Madame Duchamps."

She looked at his hand for a long moment — the universal gesture of equals, not of beggars and saviors — and took it. Her grip was firm. Her eyes were dry. She'd fallen, she'd cracked, she'd asked, and she was still standing.
"We leave tonight," Marcus said. "We have a sister to save and a fortress to defend, and we're running out of time for both."

"I'll pack the radio." Vivienne released his hand and turned to the cabinet with the efficiency of a woman who'd found her footing again. "Margaux, Henri — fifteen minutes. Essentials only. We are not taking the Kandinsky."
"*Madame—*" Margaux began.

"*No.* Art is dead weight. People are portable." She caught Marcus's eye, and something passed between them — not warmth, not yet, but recognition. The acknowledgment of one stubborn survivor by another. "I've been carrying dead weight long enough. It's time to carry something that carries back."

Elena finished with the IVs and joined Marcus at the window. Below, through the dark, she could just make out two figures in the street — Sera's silver hair catching the moonlight, Junko's compact silhouette beside her, both of them watching the building with the patient vigilance of women who'd learned that nothing good happened unguarded.
"Sixty hours until Lily's air runs out," Elena murmured. "One week until Crane. We're going to cut both close."
"We always do," Marcus said. "That's why we're still alive."

The penthouse erupted into controlled chaos — seven servants packing with the precision of people who'd been trained to prepare for emergencies, Vivienne disassembling the shortwave radio with practiced hands, Elena cataloging medical supplies. And at the center of it, Marcus stood at the window and looked out at the city he was about to cross twice more — once to save a sister, once to save a home — and felt the weight of two countdowns pressing against his ribs like hands around his throat.
The radio crackled to life on Vivienne's workbench.
Static. A voice, broken but insistent, fighting through the interference from somewhere in the mountains.
*"—Relay to any survivor—Cupid Protocol is not what you think—repeat, not what you—protect the System host—Crane is not the true—protect the—"*
The signal dissolved into static.
Vivienne looked at Marcus across the room, the radio headset in her hands, her dark eyes wide with something that had replaced fear with a colder, sharper emotion.
*Purpose.*
"It's not just Crane," she said. "There's something worse coming."
Marcus met her gaze. "Then we'd better be ready for it."
He turned from the window. "Move out in ten. Everyone stays together. Junko leads, Sera covers the rear, Elena and I take the middle. Vivienne—"
"Communications," she finished, and put on the headset like a crown.
The Penthouse Queen was leaving her tower. Not as a refugee. Not as a beggar. As an officer reporting for duty.

The art stayed on the walls. The people walked out the door. And in the rubble of the old world, something new took shape — a network of stubborn, broken, impossible people who'd chosen to carry each other instead of carrying weight that couldn't carry back.

**[PARTY STATUS UPDATE]**
**[Marcus Chen — Host, Level 12]**
**[Seraphina Vale — Zombie Queen, Stage 4 — Affection: 80]**
**[Elena Rossi — Chief Medical Officer — Affection: 65]**
**[Junko Tanaka — Downtown Guide, Stage 2 — Affection: 25]**
**[Vivienne Duchamps — Communications Officer — Affection: 35]**

**[ACTIVE EXPEDITION: The Sister's Signal — 60 hours remaining]**
**[NEW INTEL: Victor Crane assault on New Eden — ETA 7 days]**
**[NEW MYSTERY: Cupid Protocol — Origin unknown — "The System is not the error. The System is the —"]**
The countdowns ticked. The radio crackled. And Marcus Chen walked into the night with a zombie queen, a medic, a martial artist, and a woman who'd rather starve than ask — and had asked anyway.
The Romance System pulsed once, warm against his wrist.
*Good*, it seemed to say. *Now run.*
